cards.back: A class declaration in Java starts with the 'public', 'private', 'protected', or 'default' access modifier, followed by the name of the class and the '(' after the name, and finally the '{' to start the class body.
An object declaration in Java starts with the name of the class, followed by a '(' and then a list of parameters enclosed in parentheses, and finally a '{' to start the constructor body.
An object reference variable is assigned an object using the assignment operator (=), for example: Object obj = new ClassName();

Java provides four main access modifiers: public, private, protected, and default. Public methods can be accessed from anywhere, private methods can only be accessed within the same class, protected methods can be accessed within the same class and its subclasses, and default methods can be accessed within the same package.
The class body in Java consists of a sequence of statements that define the variables, methods, and other elements of the class.
